data.title: Tamboran Resources: Well Cost Reductions Play A Key Role In Unlocking Beetaloo Basin
data.description:
Tamboran Resources (TBN) is focused on exploring and commercializing natural gas production in Australia's Beetaloo Basin. The Beetaloo Basin is seen as a key future source of natural gas for Australia, although exploration/appraisal has been going on for a while and the prior main owner sold its assets there to Tamboran several years ago to focus more on green energy. The recent turmoil in the energy markets has probably further entrenched natural gas in Australia's future energy portfolio, though.

Tamboran's largest shareholder is Bryan Sheffield (formerly of Parsley Energy), who owned approximately 18% of Tamboran's shares in September 2025. Tamboran's CEO was formerly COO of Seneca Resources (an Appalachian Basin producer with around 1.2 Bcf per day of net production). It views the Beetaloo Basin as being similar to the early days (late 2000s) of U.S. shale development and is aiming for similar substantial gains in well productivity and cost improvements over time.

The premium pricing for Australian natural gas means that capital efficiency doesn't need to be similar to key U.S. natural gas plays such as the Haynesville and Marcellus. However, I believe Tamboran likely needs to get at least close to its $15 million longer-term well cost target for a 10,000-foot lateral (down from around $26 million with its recent well) to make the well economics quite solid.

If things work out well for Tamboran, it could be worth over $150 per share by 2032. However, the significant risks in terms of exploring the Beetaloo Basin, increasing production and well productivity, reducing well costs, building out infrastructure, and funding all lead me to estimate its current value at around $41 per share.

Tamboran's Fiscal Year ends on June 30. I will mention FY when referring to fiscal year, but the default will be calendar year.

Tamboran's primary listing is on the NYSE, but it also has CDIs (CHESS Depository Interests) that trade on the ASX in Australian dollars. One CDI represents 1/200th of a common share. This report also uses U.S. dollars as the main currency.

Beetaloo Basin

The Beetaloo Basin (also sometimes referred to as the Beetaloo sub-basin within the larger McArthur Basin) is located in Australia's Northern Territory. The Northern Territory is sparsely populated (under 300,000 inhabitants in an area nearly twice the size of Texas). The Beetaloo Basin is located around 300 miles away from the main city (Darwin) in the Northern Territory.

The Beetaloo Basin was previously undeveloped, but some estimates mention that there could be over 500 Tcf in natural gas resources there (although realistically only a fraction of that is ever likely to be recovered), along with the potential for near 6 Bcf per day in production.

The Beetaloo Basin is thus seen as very important to both the local natural gas supply and supplying Australia's LNG export terminals.

There are significant challenges, though, including the remoteness of the area, the need to build out infrastructure in such a remote area, and the technical issues around figuring out how to properly develop a new play.

Tamboran is the largest player in the Beetaloo Basin, with a position of around 2.9 million net acres and over 40,000 potential gross locations after its recent Falcon acquisition. This acquisition is expected to close soon, although the deadline was pushed back a bit as they work through some regulatory items.

Future Plans

Tamboran's plan is to initially supply the local Northern Territory natural gas market. This is a relatively small market, though (with average demand under 0.1 Bcf per day), so after that it will expand to supplying Australia's East Coast market. This market is much larger (at around 1 Bcf per day domestically), plus Tamboran can also help supply the Queensland LNG terminals (with export capacity that is several times more than domestic demand).

Longer-term, Tamboran is contemplating developing the integrated NTLNG export project. It previously mentioned a 2030 timeline for that project, although I believe it is likely to enter service at least a couple of years after that if it does go ahead.

Premium Natural Gas Pricing

Tamboran's natural gas production is expected to realize premium prices. It notes that the contracted price for 2027 supply to Australia's East Coast is approximately US$9.20 per Mcf, well above Henry Hub pricing (with my long-term expectation for Henry Hub being around US$3.75).

While there will be significant transportation costs (an older presentation mentioned initial costs of around US$2.70 per Mcf to access the East Coast markets before a new pipeline is built), margins should still be quite healthy due to the strong natural gas prices.

Australia's natural gas markets are heavily tied to LNG exports, with around 70% to 80% of Australia's natural gas production going to LNG exports.

Well Productivity and Costs

Tamboran mentions that the Shenandoah South pilot area wells have delivered early results in line with Marcellus wells. However, it should be noted that the data set includes a mix of older and newer wells, and newer Marcellus wells typically have considerably stronger performance than the older ones.

For example, Chesapeake Energy (as it was named at the time) mentioned an average IP30 of 26 MMCFE per day for its 2022 Marcellus wells (although those wells had an average length of close to 11,000 feet). The D&C costs for those Marcellus wells were under $1,000 per lateral foot. Those were relatively strong results, but I think an IP30 in the 15 to 20 MMCFE per day range (for a 10,000-foot well) would be reasonable to expect from an average newer Marcellus well.

Tamboran mentions recent costs around $26 million per well but is aiming to drive this down to $15 million by 2030 for a 10,000-foot lateral. This would still be well above the sub-$10 million cost for Marcellus wells, but Tamboran's operating margins per Mcf could be around double that of Marcellus wells due to the premium natural gas pricing.

Thus, if Tamboran can actually get well costs down to $15 million, a modest improvement in well productivity (from Shenandoah South 6H levels) should be pretty competitive in terms of economics.

Increasing Share Count

Tamboran has been working towards proving out its Beetaloo Basin position and has incurred significant exploration costs over the years while generating no material revenues yet. For example, it reported $137 million in incurred exploration costs in FY2025 and $76 million in incurred exploration costs in FY2024.

This has resulted in substantial cash needs, which can be seen from the following list of Tamboran's equity raises from July 2023 to June 2025. Tamboran's share count increased from 7.1 million at the start of July 2023 to 16.7 million at the end of June 2025.

This continued during the second half of 2025, with Tamboran's share count increasing to 21.1 million by the end of 2025.

As of the start of February 2026, Tamboran had 22.6 million common shares outstanding. The Falcon acquisition will add another 6.5 million shares, and its recent public offering and entitlement offers could add another 5.7 million shares.

Thus Tamboran's outstanding share count would reach around 35 million from the above items alone.

Cash Position

Tamboran mentioned that it had $138 million in pro forma cash as of early April 2026. This doesn't include the effect of cash burn during the first few months of 2026 but does include the impact of various other items, such as a recent PIPE transaction.

It aimed to raise $198 million from the public offering and entitlement offers, which should put its pro forma cash position around $330 million after factoring in offering expenses.

Tamboran used $81 million in cash for operating and investing (such as capex) activities during the second half of 2025. At that pace it would have enough cash until around early 2028 (although it wouldn't wait until its cash balance was near zero before raising more money). Tamboran is aiming to start commercial sales of natural gas later in 2026 but isn't expected to reach positive free cash flow for at least a few years.

Notes on Valuation

Tamboran is a very speculative company, with a large amount of potential, but also significant work that needs to be done to unlock that potential.

I will outline a successful scenario for Tamboran below.

Based on Australian natural gas pricing, Tamboran's EBITDA margins could end up around US$4 to US$5 per Mcf, around double that of my longer-term expectations for dry Marcellus production.

If it can ramp up net production to around 0.8 Bcf per day by 2032 (a couple of years later than its previously stated goal), that would translate into approximately $1.3 billion per year in EBITDA for Tamboran.

I believe that Tamboran could potentially maintain that production with around $600 million to $700 million in capex once its base decline moderates if it can also successfully reduce its well costs. That would leave it with around $0.6 to $0.7 billion per year in free cash flow (before income taxes), although it is expected to keep spending on production growth beyond that.

I'd value Tamboran at around $7.8 billion in 2032 in that scenario, which would be around an 8% free cash flow yield or a 6.0x EBITDA multiple. The relatively high valuation would be supported by Tamboran's large resource base and future growth potential.

I am also modeling Tamboran's future share count at 50 million in 2032, reflecting the issuance of more shares to fund its production growth. A successful scenario could thus result in Tamboran being worth $156 per share in 2032.

However, there is substantial risk with Tamboran given that it has no commercial production yet. It needs to cost-effectively grow production without too much further dilution.

A key factor to that will be bringing down its well costs. It is targeting a reduction to $15 million per well (from $26 million). If it can do that while also increasing its well productivity by around 10% to 20%, I think it will be in pretty good shape in terms of well economics. Higher costs would require stronger well results to offset that. Tamboran would also need to demonstrate that the Shenandoah South 6H well productivity can be matched or improved throughout more of its position too.

Thus, I would apply a discount rate of 25% per year to reflect the high risk and uncertainty around Tamboran. This would bring its current estimated value to around $41 per share.

There may be additional value from the NTLNG project, but I've left that out for now since so many details (funding, etc...) still need to get worked out for that.

Conclusion

Tamboran Resources is an interesting company that is trying to unlock a potentially massive amount of natural gas resources in Australia's Beetaloo Basin. If it is successful in reaching its goals (such as 0.8 Bcf per day in net production and $15 million well costs), it could be worth over $150 per share by 2032. I've given it a couple more years compared to its prior targets to reach those goals.

However, there is certainly a large amount of risk and uncertainty about its ability to reach those targets. While it nominally has enough cash to operate until early 2028 now, it will probably need to raise significant additional funds before it reaches positive cash flow. Thus, I've modeled its future share count at 50 million (up from 35 million, assuming the Falcon deal closes). This could easily be an underestimate if capital efficiency improvements are slower than expected.

As well, if Tamboran can't significantly reduce well costs, the overall well economics at Beetaloo may become marginal. Due to items such as that, I've assumed a significant 25% discount rate to reflect the risks to Tamboran achieving its targets. This would bring Tamboran's current value to around $41 per share.

Tamboran's estimated value depends a lot on this discount rate too. At a 20% discount rate, its estimated current value changes to $52, while at a 30% discount rate, it changes to $32.

data.title: Ur-Energy: From Survival To Execution, But Profitability Still Unproven
data.description:
It is time to update Ur-Energy (URG) as the situation changed since my last article. Also, what changed was that Seeking Alpha platform has issued a warning on the stock, and I very well agree with the rating. When I first covered the stock the company was unpredictable, unreliable and looked weak. The problems then were huge losses, derivative position which was causing a risk and most importantly. And also, let's add that I was not sure then whether the company will require new capital. The situation is quite different as of today, but still risky. Production is ramping up, Lost Creek is not an experiment now but a working asset. Balance is also stronger now with solid cash reserve. In other words, survival risk decreased. But now new questions arise whether these operations will become profitable.

What I was expecting in my last article happened. In 2025 production reached around 410 thousand pounds, which is practically similar to previously seen ramp up. Inventory also increased to 406 thousand pounds. This to me means, that the company is not only producing, but also has flexibility when fulfilling contracts and planning in advance. Compared to 2024 costs have decreased and the margins I think will soon increase. Contracted prices are stable, but importantly is that gross profit is positive at last. However, we cannot call it profitable company, but it is a breakthrough nonetheless. The margins being positive is a good signal and important moment, even if losses below can still be seen.

Finances have changed as well. Cash position increased to over $123 million, because they have been attractive capital through convertible notes. Part of the capital also came from warrant realisation. But this is the part which I don't like. Capital attraction most of the time leads to shareholder dilution. While, yes, we can say that for now this risk has decreased, I am still sceptical.

Operations

On operational side, the biggest change was not their growth, we expected it anyway, but how it changed the structure. Lost Creek throughout 2025 went from execution risk to questionable utilisation at least for me. I am not questioning whether they can reach that 400 thousand pounds level, but can they get economic value out of it. I might sound sceptical, but inventory growth might not be that good. Yes, it gives flexibility for contracts, but also it shows, that part of the production is not monetized immediately. And cash conversion then is still not ideal. It is a subtle, but very important difference between producing and earning.

Shirley Basin situation is not that simple too. The project is nearing its start, but the market already knows that and I believe stock has been priced for that. This means, that the upside is not guaranteed. If the costs decrease to promised $24 per pound level, this could change overall margin structure. But if not, we will just get more production volume with similar problem like now.

Financials

This is what I do not like either and to be fair, I do not feel like it changed. While they are moving forward with operations, but looking at it deeper, we can see that profitability quality is still not the best. Net loss in 2025 even got bigger, which is a bad sign to me. We can say that it came from growth phase as they need more employees, higher G&A costs, projects development. In other words, I agree that they are investing into the future, but it might come at a price to current investors.

Another important factor for me is that from accounting stand point, some of the lines are becoming better. However, cash flow is not that good to say that the business can maintain itself. Even though they have a lot of cash and in short-term I do not see any pressure, real cash burn is still ongoing. In fact it was around $43.1 million in 2025. It is just hidden by stronger capital base.

Balance and capital structure which I mentioned earlier has some trade-offs for me. Instead of going through simple share issuance, they have chosen convertible debt. On one had, this also works as a good signal that the management does not want to dilute shareholders immediately. On the other had, that cash is not free. Dilution did not disappear, it just god delayed and dependent on share price.

Convertible debt decreases immediate pressure and gives a little bit of flexibility, but for investors it poses new risk. As I said, in the future, the debt will be converted and it will become ceiling at one point, which will need to be broken. I believe that current comfort that URG is trying to achieve is not solving the problem.

Costs per unit decreased, which we can say in my opinion, that operational effectiveness is indeed improving. But the problem is that "all-in" cost per sold pound. There it might be due to inventory mix, that older costs and overall structure becomes heavier. They can produce cheaper, but when selling this effect disappears. Majority of contracts are long-term, and I can say that it gives some sort of stability. However, it caps the upside when uranium spot price is going up. Meaning that the company is not fully leveraged on the market price and at the same time nor really that secure if the price goes down. This middle part, decreases volatility, just I do not like that it limits potential profitability jump.

Overall, the company is transitioning and I cannot argue against that. But that transition needs to show that the company can actually make money.

Catalysts

Current catalysts are better than they were when I first analysed the company. I strongly believe that the market bought the story and is waiting for results, simply because since my first article the stock went up 50%. I doubt that Shirley start would cause major fluctuation in share price. But I think that the market will be moved if the start is going to be clean. Meaning that we will need to see whether they reached projected flow, whether costs are really dropping and look for any technical lags.

If in following quarters we see that the volume is actually becoming a real profitability (not just gross, but near bottom line), then the sentiment could change. If not, I simply believe that the market will start doubting this whole story. Let us not forget the uranium spot price, which like any other commodity recently started fluctuating a lot. The company itself is following uranium prices in one way or another and the share price will reflect on that. Why I am saying it is a catalyst? Because I believe that uranium spot price will remain around current levels.

Risks

The biggest risk currently that I see is margin compression. Even though we already established that the costs are decreasing I doubt that the company can absorb any pressure on its margins. Small problems could lead to increase in costs, or lower realised prices level. Given that they have operational leverage, with increasing volume, the system is becoming less flexible. Meaning that problems in logistics, technology could lead to the same margin compression problem.

Capital structure is always in my articles whenever the company is constantly increasing outstanding shares count. If the share price is going to go up, I believe we will start seeing dilution and natural ceiling to the price. If not, the company will have debt hanging around. This means that the same financial leverage which today allows company to grow without new share issuance could become a limit.

Conclusion

All in all, Ur-Energy is getting better. Operations are in good shape, at least it is in my opinion. The production stabilized, projects are moving according to guidance and Lost Creek gives foundation. I mentioned a lot their financials, and liquidity question is not that critical like was. However do not be mistaken, they raised capital just in other ways that I mentioned. Profitability has not been reached, margins while improving, still not stable enough for me to call them good.

Majority of my questions from previous articles have been answered, yet I do not feel confident in calling this company a buy. Now everything depends on whether operational basis will become real and sustainable profitability and until now, I am maintaining hold rating.

data.title: 5 Best Energy Stocks That Can Outperform After The Strait Opens
data.description:
Best Energy Stocks for a Widening Crack Spread

Oil has once again reminded investors how headline-driven the energy sector can be, with price swings tied to shifting developments in the Middle East. Tensions around Iran and the Strait of Hormuz have pushed crude higher in brief surges, only to fall quickly on signs of de-escalation. In this environment, high-beta upstream exploration and production (E&P) stocks have moved in close correlation with oil prices, which magnifies both upside and downside movements. However, a more attractive opportunity may lie further downstream.

Refiners (CRAK) Outperform Energy Sector (XLE) as Oil Falls From Highs

Refiners profit from the "crack spread," or the difference between the price of crude oil and the refined products, such as gasoline and diesel, produced from it. When oil prices peak and begin to stabilize or decline, refined product prices often remain relatively firm, allowing margins to expand. Therefore, refiners can outperform even after oil prices have rolled over, making them well worth a look amid the ongoing geopolitical headline noise. Seasonal gasoline demand, which rises as summer travel activity increases, can also reinforce our refiner stock thesis, adding a potential tailwind to prices.

According to the EIA (U.S. Energy Information Administration), the price of gas at the pump tends to peak in late summer. Therefore, even if de-escalation in Iran can bring oil prices down further, the price of gas will likely remain elevated for months.

5 Top Energy Stocks to Buy Now: Quant Strong Buys

To select the five best energy stocks to feature in this article, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and chose the pre-selected "Top Energy Stocks" list to review the highest Quant-Rated Strong Buy stocks. To narrow the search, I sorted by industry to target Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing, as well as the Integrated Oil and Gas Industries:

1. TotalEnergies SE (TTE)

  • Market Capitalization: $199.85B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 4 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 3 out of 16

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Integrated Oil and Gas

TotalEnergies capitalized on wartime disruption to make more than a $1B profit, purchasing nearly every available May-loading crude cargo produced in the United Arab Emirates and Oman during March, according to a Financial Times report. TTE is a global integrated energy company with operations spanning upstream oil and gas, refining, chemicals, and a growing LNG (liquefied natural gas) business. TTE’s diversified structure allows it to benefit from both downstream margin strength and rising demand for natural gas, especially as supply disruptions may continue long after the Iran conflict calms down. Therefore, as oil prices peak and volatility eases, TTE’s refining segment can support earnings, while LNG provides another growth driver, not only from constrained supply challenges but also from rising global electricity demand, including AI data center growth. From a Quant perspective, TTE’s Valuation and Profitability Factor grades are standouts.

TTE’s forward P/E offers a healthy discount of more than 37%, helping to support more room to run for the company’s price. Its growth-adjusted valuation metric, the forward PEG, also reveals an attractive discount, but even deeper at about 46%. A common thread tying together all five of my featured energy stocks, TTE’s Cash From Operations tells a story of fundamental strength, as its 27.34B CFO is 4,730% ahead of the sector median.

While the company’s profit trajectory looks to continue, TTE has not completely escaped Iran war damage, as its SATORP refinery complex, which it owns jointly with Saudi Aramco (ARMCO), has been temporarily shut down after sustaining damage last week in the Iran war. However, it’s also taken advantage of its strong position in the Middle East, as pointed out previously on its strategic purchase of oil in March. As the war deescalates, TTE demonstrates a return to performance momentum in a moderating oil environment, leading to a stock with a stronger refining profile.

2. Valero Energy Corporation (VLO)

  • Market Capitalization: $72.37B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 16 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 2 out of 18

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing

Former Alpha Picks stock, which helped fuel the portfolio, Valero Energy is one of the largest independent petroleum refiners in the world. Based in the U.S., VLO operates refineries across North America and the U.K. The company is a direct beneficiary of widening crack spreads as it converts oil into gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel. In an environment where oil prices spike and retreat on headlines, VLO can outperform as refining margins widen when oil stabilizes or declines, benefiting once again from the high demand and supply chain disruptions like it saw amid the conflict between Russia and Ukraine when it was added to the Alpha Picks portfolio in May 2022. The summer travel season further supports demand for gas and jet fuel, further supporting what's already an attractive Quant perspective.

While VLO’s 'D' Valuation Grade appears to be its only weak Quant Factor, key metrics reveal a different story, especially when considering its forward growth potential. For example, VLO’s growth-adjusted valuation, the forward PEG, is attractively low at 0.44, which is nearly a 65% discount to the sector median. This leads to the company’s solid growth metrics, which have the potential to improve in the coming quarter if VLO can capitalize on a widening crack spread.

Growth metrics that support VLO’s solid growth scorecard include its forward EBITDA Growth of nearly 12%, which outpaces the sector median by more than 121%, and its long-term growth durability, as measured by the EPS Long-Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) of more than 26%. This metric is close to 180% of VLO’s sector peers. With strong growth providing healthy free cash flow, Valero is well positioned to lead the sector even after oil peaks, and this transitions to another refiner with added midstream stability.

3. Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC)

  • Market Capitalization: $65.56B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 42 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 5 out of 18

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing

Marathon Petroleum is a leading U.S. refiner with approximately 3 million barrels per day of crude oil capacity across 13 refineries, with exposure to midstream operations through its MPLX (MPLX) subsidiary. This combination can enhance earnings strength, especially as refining margins widen when crude input costs ease. Like VLO, MPC was an Alpha Picks stock that returned double-digit gains to the portfolio and stands to benefit if seasonal demand for gas and jet fuel is strong during peak travel months. Also similar to our first featured energy stock, MPC still has room to run in price, based on key valuation metrics, growth potential, and sector-leading cash flow.

MPC’s valuation/growth combination is nearly identical to VLO’s, beginning with the attractive forward PEG valuation metric of 0.44, revealing a growth-adjusted 65% discount to the sector. While Marathon also has a solid growth grade (B+) like VLO, I’m highlighting profitability here for MPC to demonstrate a few key metrics important to refiners.

An under-appreciated but highly relevant profitability metric is the Asset Turnover Ratio, which is 1.64x for MPC and nearly 282% ahead of sector peers. This tells investors that MPC is good at moving large volumes, generating revenue, and utilizing its assets. This can indicate the ability to maximize output when crack spreads are strong. The more important profitability metric, Cash From Operations, is 8.25B, over 14x bigger than the sector median, confirming its margins are real. As oil price swings calm down and gas demand remains strong, Marathon offers a balanced way to take advantage of downstream strength, leading into a smaller, mid-cap refiner with greater margin growth potential.

4. Par Pacific Holdings, Inc. (PARR)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.26B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 13 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 1 out of 18

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing

One of my current Alpha Picks holdings, Parr Pacific, is a mid-cap refining and logistics company with operations in niche markets, including Hawaii and the U.S. West Coast. The company’s geographic location allows it to benefit from larger refining margins when regional supply tightens, making it a high-beta play on crack spread widening. PARR’s impressive growth metrics and sector-leading momentum make it a compelling energy stock to hold in the current environment.

PARR's impressive forward 'A+' Growth Factor grade helps to explain how it is a top Quant-rated stock in the energy sector and number one in the Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing industry. Its forward EBITDA growth of 36% is more than 6x the sector median, while forward Operating Cash Flow Growth is above 97%, which is about 14x its sector peers. In an environment where oil prices stabilize or decline while gas and jet fuel demand rise seasonally, Par Pacific is well positioned to continue outperforming its larger sector peers (XLE).

PARR’s growth and momentum make it a good complement to larger refiners, which leads to an integrated energy stock with global exposure.

5. Repsol, S.A. (REPYY)

  • Market Capitalization: $28.16B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 11 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 7 out of 16

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Integrated Oil and Gas

Repsol is an integrated energy company based in Spain with exposure to refining, upstream production, and a growing low-carbon energy segment. Its refining business plays a central role in the current environment, where strong seasonal demand for gasoline and diesel supports margins even as crude prices fluctuate. Repsol’s investments in renewable energy also provide long-term growth potential as global energy demand evolves post-Iran conflict.

Beginning with valuation, REPYY’s forward 5.43 P/E is at a steep discount to the sector median’s 13.40, and the forward PEG of 0.30 is even steeper at more than 75%. Digging deeper, Repsol’s attractive forward EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA suggest the market is undervaluing its earnings power and cash flow potential, which leads to key profitability metrics.

As you may detect from REPYY's 'B+' Profitability Grade and seemingly average to slightly-below-average margins, the key metrics contributing to its higher overall score are Cash From Operations growth. The CFO growth of 6.30B is over 1,000% higher than the sector median, and this translates into Free Cash Flow, a core metric for fundamental strength. With a balanced mix of upstream and downstream operations, Repsol rounds out a group of companies well positioned for outperformance, which segues nicely into our conclusion.

Conclusion: Crack Spreads, LNG Exposure Can Outperform

Energy markets remain volatile, but sector opportunities are shifting downstream as refining margins may soon begin to drive performance rather than oil prices. Companies with strong exposure to crack spreads, supported by gas and jet fuel demand through the coming travel season, appear well positioned to outperform even as oil stabilizes or declines. At the same time, integrated companies with LNG exposure can provide additional balance through demands tied to global energy needs, as well as AI-related growth. Together, these top energy stocks offer a solid mix of cash flow strength, earnings potential, and upside momentum, giving investors a means of navigating headline noise and a post-war energy environment.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Subscribe to Premium for unlimited access to Seeking Alpha quant ratings and expert content. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Feel free to check out our new Alpha Picks app in the Apple App Store! Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

Happy investing!

data.title: Goldman Sachs Q1 Results Flag Risks From Middle East Conflict And Inflation
data.description:
The market’s reaction to Goldman Sachs’ (GS) first quarter (Q1) earnings for FY2026 – made on the 13th of April 2026 – was striking: despite early trends that the company will repeat the results of Fiscal Year 2025 wherein net earnings registered strong growth over the previous year’s, the stock went on to fall by 2%.

There are two specific factors that could be attributed to this immediate conviction – one for the industry that the company operates in and one more generalized for the economy.

Trend Drilldown

In recent years, Goldman Sachs has been working on transitioning out of the consumer banking business altogether. Early in January, the company announced that it will be transitioning its Apple Card business – which represented the first consumer credit card by Goldman Sachs – to Chase over a period of 2 years. In 2025, it had entirely exited the General Motors card business as well.

Meanwhile, some line items have been merging as the company streamlines its business. For example, “Equity Investments” and “Debt Investments” are now reported in aggregate as the bank transitions from direct investments on its balance sheet to a scaled third-party funds-driven business. Previously, the bank would use its own balance sheet to make massive investments (known as “Principal Investments”) into opportunities. Going forward, it would increasingly raise money from outside investors and manage the investment in exchange for “Management Fees” and “Incentive Fees”. The line item merge in this instance is a signal of commitment to the market.

While the changes don’t alter the bottom line (i.e. earnings) or the top line (i.e. revenue) in past quarters, it does alter the horizon of the trend line analysis relative to more recent data.

One potentially significant standout in trends is in the company’s steadfast commitment to compensation and benefits trending in tandem with top- and bottom-line changes. This is a tacit recognition that banks are less about technology or infrastructure and more about the people, be they an analyst in the operations team or a high-profile wealth advisor. When the bank does well – as a general rule – those within do well and effort will be expended to retain the best among them.

In terms of revenue share, key business segments and line items are seemingly a mixed bag, albeit with a positive overall tone:

A strong pipeline in completed mergers & acquisitions (M&A) volume along with higher revenues in prime financing and “intermediation” – making markets, executing orders, and clearing transactions for clients –led the “Equities” segment within the Global Banking and Markets (GBM) division to rise 31% relative to Q1 of 2025. The Asset and Wealth Management (AWM) division was up 10% over Q1 2025 on the back of strong revenues from management and other fees.

Within GBM, the “Fixed Income, Currency and Commodities” (FICC) segment showed a 10% decline relative to Q1 2025 due to significantly lower net revenues in interest rate products, mortgages and credit products. Meanwhile, AWM’s “Private Banking and Lending” segment – which provides loans to wealth management clients against commercial and residential real estate, securities or other assets while raising deposits through said clients and Marcus – registered 12% lower than in Q1 2025. Amidst an overall positive picture for the company, these fact patterns stood out amidst market sentiment on numerous indicators.

Credit “Cockroaches” and Sticky Inflation

In October 2025, JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon created a stir during his company's earnings call when he flagged the implosion of auto lender Tricolor Holdings and car-parts supplier First Brands Group as a crisis event for private credit. “I probably shouldn’t say this, but when you see one cockroach, there are probably more”, he told analysts on the call. “Everyone should be forewarned on this one.”

The term “cockroaches” rapidly went on to become a term employed across numerous media releases by the financial services industry. In Q1 2025, Jamie Dimon’s warning wasn’t exactly unfounded: private credit markets faced a record surge in redemption requests totalling over $20 billion, up 183% from Q4 2025.

Major firms, including Blue Owl, BlackRock and Carlyle, capped withdrawals at 5% to manage liquidity, leaving roughly $7 billion in requests unmet. This highlights the increasing strain on the $2 trillion sector – which has a network of complex linkages with global investment banks.

In terms of size of redemption requests, Goldman Sachs was the largest fund manager that honoured all redemption requests, which were a little under 5% of the fund’s value. In related filings within the SEC’s Edgar system, Goldman Sachs’ Private Credit platform stated that while it did see a 7% decline in overall quarterly inflows into its private credit fund in Q4 2025, this was a more moderate decrease relative to most of their peers. Furthermore, December inflows were 11% higher than its YTD ("Year-Till-Date") average, the Q4 2025 redemption rate of 3.5% was lower than their peers who averaged above 5% and the ratio of inflows to redemptions was 3.3x. The company asserts that “uncertain markets provoke a flight to quality in investors” and that February 2026 was its platform’s second-highest subscription month since its inception.

In Conclusion

Goldman Sachs’ provision for credit losses rose nearly 10% from a year earlier to $315 million, marking its largest increase in loan loss provisions since 2020. During the earnings call, CEO David Solomon stated that while the environment for mergers and other deals has been resilient, the war in the Middle East is a point of concern to watch for. The resulting high prices of commodities could future capital markets deals like mergers or debt issuance while inflation trends will impact numerous other parts of the market that the company operates in. Solomon also said that market churn from the war had cooled IPO listings in March in particular.

As the earnings performance indicates, the company has diverse pools of revenue generation which can act as offsets or multipliers. Inflation – which has remained sticky particularly in the U.S. – is a potential risk for the company going forward as it generates 60% of its revenue from the Americas. If inflation stays elevated, consumption is impaired. Furthermore, the Fed’s rate is likely to stay “higher for longer”, which makes financing deals more expensive and the servicing of debt more burdensome. Thus, inflation becomes something of a “two-headed” beast for the likes of U.S.’ top global banks.

If inflation remains sticky and the Fed rate stays “higher for longer”, the recessionary outlook strengthens. This is when financial services stocks typically attract higher interest. While banks do get hit by recessionary effects, their historied swift and surgical approach to limiting costs and pivoting towards less risky activities – which having a diversified pool of revenues helps in doing – typically mean that they are affected less and recover faster. On top of that, Goldman Sachs also pays dividends, which tend to be another plus of investors. Goldman’s board declared that a quarterly dividend of $4.50 will paid out towards the end of June.

All in all, while markets are reacting to the potential gloom in the macro environment painted in the picture presented by the bank, the market’s immediate reaction was certainly premature; the market went on to add 2.11% to the stock’s price on the 14th of April – potentially in recognition of both the diversified revenue base and the value of holding on the stock on basis of the aforementioned merits. Nonetheless, it bears noting that no stock exists in a bubble. if markets turn bearish, Goldman Sachs’ stock would likely see volatility.

data.title: V.F. Corporation: A Decent Job Done, But I'm Not Excited Yet
data.description:
It’s been well over two years since the last time I wrote about V.F. Corporation (VFC). I said in that article that the new CEO has a lot of work to do. Since assigning the company a hold rating, the share price has appreciated by only around 15.6%, whereas if you were to buy the broader index like SPY (SPY), you’d be up over 44% on your investment. At first glance, it appears that the CEO hasn’t done anything for the shareholders thus far. Let’s dig into the overall performance of the company.

Performance

Let’s start from the top. Clearly, there has been some sort of trouble regarding its top-line performance, as its revenues fell from $11.33B to $9.58B. The management attributed the weakness to underperforming brands like Vans and Dickies, specifically calling them out as drags on performance. In some quarters over the last three years or so, these two brands experienced double-digit declines in sales, with Vans being much more of a problem since it is a much bigger brand than Dickies. Also, in the latest quarters, we can see that the company started to see some sort of recovery in sales, suggesting we may have seen the bottom.

In terms of positives, The North Face brand was the most consistent performer throughout. In the latest quarter, the brand continued to see growth of around 8%, while Vans continued to see sales declines, although it wasn’t as bad as before, with a refresh in products and digital performance improvements. And now with the sale of Dickies to Bluestar Alliance, the company has gotten rid of one of the bad-performing brands. Regionally, the Americas showed the strongest performance in the last three years.

Let’s go over VFC’s profitability in the last three years. Here, we can see that when the then-new CEO, Bracken Darrell, took over the leadership, he got a company that was kind of in shambles. Margins were contracting significantly in the first year that he took over; however, it seems that whatever he did in mid-2024 began to work as profitability reversed course, and in the latest quarters, VFC became a profitable company once again. Within the first year and a half, he went to work with the “Reinvent” transformation program, targeting at least $300m in gross cost savings by FY25, and $500-$600m of net operating income expansion by the end. I think this was quite a classic restructuring strategy that new CEOs tend to do. Clean up the portfolio, fix the pricing/channel mix, and focus on a tighter cost discipline. So far, it looks like it paid off.

We can see the same recovery in the company’s other key profitability and efficiency metrics. Although they are still not great, the uptrend gives me hope that the company will see much better returns in the long run. I’d like to see returns going back to double-digits, as they were last seen back in the beginning of 2019.

With the reinvent program, the CEO said he would like to get the company’s leverage down to 2.5x or below, so let’s take a look at the financial position in the latest quarter. VFC finished Q3 ’26 with around $1.466B in cash and equivalents, against $3.56B in long-term debt. The company is still on the hook for a lot of debt, but its cash position is also quite strong. Its operating income improved from last year, while the debt slightly came down as well, leading to an interest coverage ratio of around 7x, which is quite healthy. For reference, many analysts believe that an interest coverage ratio of 2x is healthy. I like to see at least 5x because I’m more conservative like that, and even then, the company is much stronger. So, I don’t think the leverage is an issue. Some investors hate debt overall, so I believe the management will continue to work on it. At the start of the year, the company’s leverage ratio was 4.1x, and now they are guiding to bring it down to 3.5x or below by the end of FY26, so there is still a way to go.

Overall, it seems that there has been some progress regarding the company’s progress under the new CEO, who is not so new any longer. He has been quite proactive when it comes to cutting out the fat, streamlining operations, and focusing on what will be the big drivers of performance going forward.

Comments on the Outlook

So, what are the main drivers that will continue to help VFC improve going forward? I like that Bracken Darrell is focusing on what is important in terms of brands. The two best-performing brands remain The North Face and Timberland. They are seen as the growth and profit engines for 2026, and most likely beyond. These brands have been growing mid-to high-single digits, and being of higher quality, demanding higher prices, carry much higher margins. The mix shit needs to continue towards these brands.

I’ve always seen DTC as a very attractive model, so the management paying attention to how this progresses will also be vital to improving operations in the future. The company already said that DTC “re-inflected” growth, so the management will continue to nurture the model to achieve higher margins and profitability overall.

The big elephant in the room remains the Vans brand. The management needs to clean out the shop and execute another multi-year brand reset, which would position the brand at the forefront of innovation in footwear. It doesn’t have to be too experimental in terms of design. Just introduce a cleaner product that would attract the younger generations, as it did when I was wearing them 15 years ago. It seems the brand lost its relevance and is in dire need of a reset. The brand dropped the ball and stopped innovating, and focused on a handful of archival icons like Old Skool and checkerboard slip-ons. It forgot to innovate, and that is why it started to lag behind the competitors.

The management said that stabilization of Vans will be one of the biggest swing factors for medium-term growth, but execution is going to be important here. The brand lost its cool factor, and that is sad.

Valuation

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model to see what the company might be worth. Below are the assumptions I used.

For revenue growth, analysts expect a lot of variance, so to keep it simple, I went with quite a low growth of 3% for the next 5 years, which seems achievable. For profitability, I am assuming the company will see improvements as price mix shifts towards the better-performing brands like The North Face and Timberland. For the rest of the model, I went with the company’s historical averages. I also went with the company’s WACC of 9% as my discount rate, so there is no margin of safety, and 2.5% terminal growth rate. So, with these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And with these projections, we see that VFC’s fair value is around $17.67, which means it is still trading at a premium to its fair value.

Closing Comments

In the last year, the company’s share price has increased by around 68%, outperforming the SPY. Clearly, the shareholders loved the CEO’s effort to turn the company around. I just don’t think I am going to invest in it right now, because the growth is still relatively weak, and the Vans brand continues to affect the overall performance. The debt levels are still high, but sustainable, and I like what the management has done with the company as well. It just doesn’t excite me all that much.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Your Tech Sector Rorschach Test
data.description:
By Mike Larson

You’ve heard of a Rorschach test, right? The one where you’re shown a series of inkblot images - and your answers are used as a psychological analysis tool? Well today, it’s time for your tech sector test!

Here’s my MoneyShow Chart of the Day - a nine-month chart of the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). Do you think it’s bullish? Or bearish?

Invesco QQQ Trust

(Source: StockCharts)

If you’re a bull, you’ll probably point to the recent 10-day winning streak and the fact the QQQs are back to roughly breakeven on the year. If you’re a bear, you’ll probably highlight how the QQQs have gone nowhere since November, and that we’ve been rallying on steadily falling volume.

I didn’t show the charts here. But bulls could also point to the fact the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) is near its old highs... while bears could say the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) and SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF (DIA) remain well below their pre-war peaks.

So, what do you see? Something to get excited about? Or a market that still hasn’t proved itself?

Make sure you can answer that question to your own satisfaction before committing more capital here. From where I sit though, it looks like the jury is still out.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: ENOR: Unsurprising Beneficiary Of The Iran War
data.description:
The iShares MSCI Norway ETF (ENOR) shows how well Norway is placed with its pretty heavy economic skew towards select industries given the outbreak of the Iran War and the energy crisis at its route. Norway has three claims to fame:

  • Salmon
  • Oil (primary)
  • Missiles

Relative to the energy crisis, salmon isn't particularly impressive. While technically a consumer staple, it is a commodity that is subject to price changes and value changes of these companies' biological reserves, and as a superior protein can be downtraded in an inflationary environment.

What impresses about ENOR and Norway is that it's so heavy on oil E&P but also oil services, stocks which have all seen major demand given the Iran War and stranded supply risks for the Gulf states. With the ceasefire talks falling apart for the moment and a blockade in place, the conflict has not made progress to resolution other than speculation that a second round of talks might occur and that Iran is more responsive now. Moreover, Kongsberg (KBGGY) is an important exposure that continues to gain traction with the European defense spending inflection but also from the US, being an F-35 partner among other things. Greater conflict risk supports the equity.

We also think the insurance exposures as part of financials are benefiting from yield curve dynamics. Banks are more iffy. Financials are the second largest allocation in ENOR. The NOK has held on despite its risk-on character thanks to oil fundamentals.

But reinflation in other markets with less marginal currencies may eventually sideline the NOK. Moreover, the war as the principal factor leading to the positive ENOR performance may unwind, and this would not be the optimal time for the ETF as it is correlated with spot prices and E&P and oil services. It's a sensitive bet now that it's already run-up and so dependent on imminent headlines around the war.

ENOR Breakdown

Sectorally, energy sits at the top with exposures like Equinor (EQNR) and to a lesser extent Aker BP (AKRBF). While technically a materials play, they have exposure to fertiliser through Yara (YARIY) which is also correlated to supply of petroleum product and has shot up in the current crisis.

Other than that, financial exposures are considerable, with just DNB (DNBBF) at over 11%, a stock we've covered in the past and have always liked. There are also insurance exposures like Storebrand (SREDF) and Gjensidige (GJNSF).

Industrials is driven primarily by Kongsberg, the defense and missile exposure.

Consumer staples are consumer products companies like Orkla and also salmon farmers.

We can discuss each of these sectors in the bull and bear case for the ETF.

Bull Case

  • The ceasefire talks have fallen through, and the blockade could increase conflict risk and has already lead to aborted transits. Oil supply could fall once again after a breather in the weekend. This is good especially for oil E&P which is indexed towards spot prices, with Norwegian capacity entirely unhindered by the conflict.
  • Financial exposures are benefiting from yield curve dynamics especially the insurers. Higher short term rates and longer term rates will allow for duration lengthening to drive higher reserve portfolio returns, and short term rollovers will be yielding at higher spots.
  • Defense stocks should be seen positively in situations where there is more conflict.
  • Consumer staples are not the worst consumer exposure to have, and are less discretionary.

Bear Case

  • If the blockade is successful, it could spur new talks that could lead to a renewed possibility of diplomatically ending the conflict. An end to the war would assuage oil markets and cost E&P in terms of lower spot prices.
  • While insurers are alright in the current environment, banks have to deal with possibly more reserving requirements and also a flattening of the yield curve. Short term rate expectations are those that are rising, and therefore could put upward pressure on funding costs without as much benefit to returns on longer-term lending. This is a consequence of the general reinflation from higher prices at the pump as well as associated wage-price spiral effects. Moreover, with higher rates coming in for more attractive currency pairs as well, the NOK has a tendency to get sidelined for less marginal yielding currencies and we think that could materialise if reinflation persists making the previously stickier Norwegian inflation less unique as inflation becomes reintroduced everywhere. If the war ends the NOK may even do alright, as it has a risk-on character more than being correlated to fundamentals of oil according to academic research.
  • Consumer staples, particularly salmon, may be subject to downtrading in the event of inflation led demand destruction in favour of inferior proteins.

Bottom Line

The case is a little mixed. Financials are seeing a flattening yield curve but also better return dynamics for the reserve portfolios given the war and assuming it lasts. Oil is clearly benefiting from the energy crisis, as well as defense from the war risks globally. On balance, the most significant exposure being energy positions ENOR negatively if the war ends soon. If it doesn't, profits will be considerable for E&P and the conditions will start to become evident for the oil services stocks to be able to get new mandates driven by downstream CAPEX. Persistent global reinflation prevents the stickier inflation of Norway from getting the NOK's yield to stand out and also brings a risk-off attitude that the NOK can be sensitive to. The end of an energy crisis could mean the NOK goes back to standing out a bit as a high yield developed market currency. It could go any way at this point, depends on the war.

data.title: Guardian Pharmacy Services: Waiting For A Better Entry Point
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for Guardian Pharmacy Services (GRDN) is a hold rating. I like the business. GRDN has a decent moat, and the IRA headwind appears manageable. The problem is valuation. A lot of the good news already looks priced in, and I do not think the current multiple leaves enough room for upside. For now, I would wait for a better entry point.

Business Overview

GRDN is a US-based long-term care pharmacy services provider. It operates a network of local pharmacies across 38 states and serves residents of assisted living facilities, behavioral health facilities, and skilled nursing facilities. The business model focuses on dispensing and delivering medications to long-term care residents and providing related services such as clinical consulting, compliance packaging, billing and administrative support, etc.

Guardian is in the right part of long-term care

I believe the most important aspect of GRDN’s equity story is that more than two-thirds of its revenue comes from assisted living facilities and behavioral health facilities, not skilled nursing. I call this out because assisted living is a more difficult operating environment for pharmacy providers. In assisted living, residents often have the right to choose their own pharmacy, unlike skilled nursing, where the facility typically uses a single provider. At the same time, residents are older, take many medications, and live in facilities with limited clinical staff. This makes medication coordination harder, and this gap creates room for a specialized operator to come in (like GRDN). We know that this is a problem to be solved because GRDN is now serving more than 205,000 residents across approximately 8,200 facilities through 54 pharmacies, with approximately 140,000 of those residents in assisted living alone. Notably, there is a 90% adoption rate in partnered assisted living facilities.

But the point is not that GRDN has size; the point is that it has achieved significant scale in this niche market. In my view, the operating landscape is split two ways: (1) skilled nursing is more mature, more contract-driven, and more constrained; and (2) assisted living, in contrast, is less standardized and gives pharmacy providers (like GRDN) more room to differentiate themselves (be it through service, coordination, execution, etc.). In the context for GRDN, this means it is not relying on overall market growth to grow. It is operating in a market where better execution can translate into more facility wins, higher adoption, and deeper relationships over time. In other words, this is a market where GRDN can reinvest aggressively to grow faster because assisted living is still a setting where customers will choose the provider that solves the most problems.

The moat

GRDN’s moat is not just distribution. It is the fact that GRDN is built into how assisted living facilities actually run (i.e., GRDN is embedded into the daily workflow). Over the years, GRDN has built tools around workflows (e.g., cost control, medication management, clinical oversight, etc.), with its most famous tools such as Guardian Compass, which helps with planning, purchasing, and operational control; and GuardianShield, which helps to garner insights from clients' data. Aside from that, you also have plenty of other tools like the Insurance Optimizer Program, the Antibiotic Stewardship Program, the Psychotropic Medication Reduction Program, etc. I do not think it is necessary to go through every tool, but the Insurance Optimizer Program is worth focusing on because it shows why GRDN is harder to replace.

Based on my understanding, the Insurance Optimizer Program works in two steps. First, it helps residents choose the right pharmacy benefit plan and navigate annual enrollment. Second, once prescriptions are being filled, GRDN works with physicians and insurance payors to get non-covered drugs approved, resolve rejected claims, and, when needed, find an alternative that is covered. Both residents and facilities would also receive reports to show how much they have saved. This may seem simple, but it serves a great value proposition to both residents and facilities. The residents want it for a simple reason: lower out-of-pocket cost and less hassle. For facilities, the value is enjoying a lower administrative burden, fewer billing-related issues to deal with, and a simpler medication process for staff to manage. For context, in 2025, this program saved residents >$56 million, and since it is working nicely, facilities are unlikely to switch, and that is a moat I like to see.

IRA is a headwind, but not a dealbreaker

The main pushback on GRDN is the change to the Inflation Reduction Act [IRA], which affects drug pricing economics. This is a real risk, and the revenue hit is real. But I don’t think investors should be super worried. While the expected 2026 impact is ~$150 million of revenue and ~$5 million to $7 million of EBITDA, management still kept 2026 revenue guidance at $1.4 billion to $1.42 billion and still raised adj. EBITDA guidance to $120 million to $124 million. Notice that while revenue got hit, profit guidance actually went up, so that tells us GRDN's underlying economics didn’t really see a big impact. In fact, it tells us that GRDN has levers it can pull to drive up margins. For instance, GRDN has already built direct relationships with payer partners and has been in discussions with Pharmacy Benefit Managers [PBMs]. This should translate to better economics since this stronger direct relationship can give GRDN a better chance to explain its value provided to residents, facilities, and health plans. So, while the IRA is clearly a headwind, it is not a dealbreaker. In my view, the more important takeaway is that GRDN’s business model is resilient enough to absorb the reset and still grow profit.

There is also a competitive angle to this. This IRA rule change that hurts the sector does not hit every operator the same way. Smaller independents have fewer tools, less negotiating power, and less room to absorb this hit. GRDN, while being impacted, really isn’t going to feel much of a big pain after this reset. The moat is still strong, and it still has scale. If anything, this puts GRDN in a better position to win market share as smaller players exit the market once their unit economics turn less attractive.

Valuation

The only problem with GRDN is valuation isn’t cheap. The stock trades at ~30x NTM PE with ~10% revenue growth CAGR once we get past this IRA reset. It is not that attractive when we compare it to Brightspring Health Services (BTSG), which trades at 27x NTM PE but has a mid-teens revenue growth outlook. Even if we adjust for the fact that GRDN has a higher margin profile vs. BTSG, I think that is already well reflected in the ~3x multiple premium. On a historical basis, GRDN is also trading at the higher end of its valuation range (average is 26x), so I am not confident that multiples will see any more re-rating from here. As such, I prefer to wait for a cheaper multiple before I can give this a buy rating.

Risk

GRDN’s workflow-driven moat may be less sticky than I thought. If tools like Insurance Optimizer, GuardianShield, and related clinical programs stop delivering clear savings, facilities may become less willing to push adoption higher. Another risk is execution from scaling. This is not a simple distribution business. Any compliance issue or medication error could damage customer trust quickly and structurally impair GRDN’s growth profile.

Conclusion

Fundamentally, I view GRDN as a high-quality operator in a niche where execution matters. It has achieved scale, has a nice moat, and has the ability to protect margins through the IRA reset. I also think GRDN can keep winning share over time, especially if smaller players feel more pressure. That said, I do not think investors need to chase the stock here. The business is good, but the valuation is not cheap.

data.title: Inside Today's Mortgage Credit Opportunity
data.description:
Daniel J. Ivascyn: During the global financial crisis, when I think you started with us back in '08, you had major problems with mortgage lending and regulator's response when the crisis began to subside was significant regulation, making it very, very hard to extend credit in an irresponsible fashion.

That's in contrast to some of the issues that we're seeing on the underwriting side in unsecured corporate credit that made it through the global financial crisis relatively unscathed.

It's been years of very, very strong fundamental performance. Because of this conservatism, both in terms of home building as well as credit extension, there's a tremendous amount of equity trapped in the home. That's great as a lender. So we see this as one of the most attractive absolute and relative value opportunities in the market. And we have deep resources here.

Jason Steiner: As policy rates rose across the world, consumers benefited from having opted into a 30-year fixed rate loan at three, three and a half percent. And so, that created a very unique dynamic for the US consumer. As rates rose, that largest liability that they had on their balance sheet, right, the cost of that fund didn't rise at all. And as income rose, that created additional lending opportunities into the consumer debt markets.

And the other kind of long-term impact that we've seen from the long-term locked-in rate is a reluctance to give up that liability, right? That liability, in some sense, is an asset. That's created an environment where lending into the second lien market is starting to evolve.

It's starting to become an option for consumers because, again, as a refinance tool for credit cards or auto loans or the type of consumer debt that comes at typically double-digit-type yields, having the opportunity to cash out some of the equity in your home could bring down that consumer's overall monthly payment obligations, which will lead to any of the even healthier consumers if the economy slows or if the economy starts to weaken.

Daniel J. Ivascyn: And I think the second important theme to note is the public policy focus on housing affordability. This should create significant performance tailwinds in opportunity. A recent example is the federal housing agencies deciding to acquire mortgage collateral on the order of a couple hundred billion dollars, at least in the base case.

So I think it's always important to pair the more quantitative type data analysis risk management systems with an appreciation for the policy uncertainty. And over the multiple decades we've operated in these markets, we've certainly seen a lot of policy twists and turns.

Jason Steiner: Generally speaking, I think we would find the opportunity to invest in seasoned mortgage credit to be even more compelling. Today, you're buying loans that would generally trade at a discount; they were originated in a different interest rate environment.

So those low-coupon mortgages come at a dollar price that's generally below par, not because it's a reflection of weak credit, but because prevailing rates are just a lot higher that eliminates some of some convexity risk creates some longer-duration-type profiles that maybe are not the best asset for other investors.

It gets a little bit more of a narrow focus investor base. So all those opportunities create an environment where we could pick up, spread, earn returns and still be a little bit more defensive at a time when the go-forward outlook is more uncertain.

data.title: Artisan Mid Cap Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Management

Matt Kamm, CFA

Portfolio Manager (Co-Lead)

Jason White, CFA

Portfolio Manager (Co-Lead)

Jim Hamel, CFA

Portfolio Manager

Angela Wu

Portfolio Manager

Jay Warner, CFA

Portfolio Manager

Investing Environment

Global equities declined during Q1 2026, pulling back from all-time highs as markets responded to a combination of geopolitical escalation, shifting monetary policy expectations and heightened macroeconomic uncertainty. Energy stocks surged, with Q1 returns exceeding 30% across major indices. Value outperformed growth as investors continued to rotate out of technology stocks and into more commodity-sensitive areas.

Market leadership continued to broaden during the period. In the US, large-cap growth stocks lagged, while mid- and small-cap indices were more resilient, posting modest gains for the quarter. The best performing segment of the market was the Russell 2000® Value Index, which delivered a 5.0% return during Q1. Outside the US, emerging markets continued their recent streak of outperformance relative to developed markets. Europe declined, while other developed markets posted mixed results.

Bond markets experienced increased volatility as rising energy prices and shifting monetary policy expectations drove a sharp increase in 10-year Treasury yields, which spiked to 4.4% in late March. Yields later moderated as investors weighed slowing growth against persistent inflation pressures. At its March monetary policy meeting, the Federal Reserve held rates steady and updated its projections to reflect slower growth and somewhat higher inflation. While the Fed continued to characterize the economy as resilient, it emphasized elevated uncertainty and signaled a more cautious, data-dependent approach to policy easing. At the start of Q1, markets expected one or two more rate cuts by the Fed in 2026, but that changed to a modest probability of policy tightening as markets moved to a more balanced view of inflation and growth.

The escalation of conflict in Iran became a central driver of market behavior. Military actions and subsequent retaliations contributed to a sharp rise in oil prices and disrupted supplies of other important industrial inputs. Renewed commodity-driven inflation pressures increased market sensitivity to macroeconomic developments and reinforced a rotation toward commodity-sensitive and defensive parts of the equity market, while adding further pressure to growth-oriented sectors.

Artificial intelligence (AI)-related investment remained an important support for capital spending and corporate earnings. However, investor sentiment toward AI-related equities was uneven, with periodic reassessments of valuation, capital intensity and return potential contributing to increased dispersion within the technology sector. Expanding AI capabilities sent software shares tumbling as investors reassessed business models, competitive positioning and long-term monetization potential. These developments also prompted broader questions around valuation and sustainability across both public and private software markets, contributing to increased volatility within the industry. At the same time, leadership within AI has shifted toward infrastructure-oriented segments such as memory and optical components, which continue to benefit from sustained data center investment. While the mega-cap AI winners that have driven market returns in recent periods lagged during Q1, AI-related spending trends remain intact, supporting continued demand across the broader ecosystem.

Economic data reflected a mixed global backdrop. While US economic activity remained relatively resilient, there were signs of softening in the labor market alongside a notable decline in consumer sentiment. Outside the US, growth remained uneven, with the outlook for Europe revised down following the escalation of conflict and conditions across Asia looking more mixed. Taken together, these dynamics reinforced a more cautious tone in markets as the quarter unfolded.
